title: Shame & Guilt
lead: Guilt says 'I did something bad'. Shame says 'I am bad'. Knowing the difference is the beginning of setting yourself free from carrying both.
explanation: Guilt and shame feel similar but work very differently. Guilt is about behaviour — 'I did something that goes against my values'. Used well, it can guide us to repair and do better. Shame is about identity — 'I am flawed, unworthy, unlovable'. Shame rarely motivates change; it makes us hide, shrink, and give up.

Chronic shame often traces back to messages we absorbed — from harsh criticism, trauma, or comparison — that told us we were the problem. It can feel like the truth, but it's a wound, not a fact about your worth.

You can honour genuine guilt (by making amends and adjusting course) while gently releasing toxic shame (by challenging the belief that you are fundamentally bad). Self-compassion, not self-punishment, is what actually helps people change and heal.
symptoms: Feeling you don't deserve good things

immediate.title: Separate the behaviour from the self
steps: Notice: am I telling myself 'I did bad' or 'I am bad'?
Reframe shame into guilt: 'I made a mistake' rather than 'I am a mistake'
A mistake is something you did, not something you are

immediate.title: Speak to yourself as a friend
immediate.description: Ask what you'd say to someone you love who did the same thing. Offer yourself that same understanding — not to excuse harm, but to stay human enough to grow.

immediate.title: Bring shame into the light
immediate.description: Shame thrives in secrecy. Telling a safe, non-judgemental person the thing you feel ashamed of often dissolves much of its power.

longTerm.title: Practise self-forgiveness
longTerm.description: Self-forgiveness isn't letting yourself off the hook — it's taking responsibility, making amends where you can, and then choosing to stop punishing yourself endlessly.

longTerm.title: Self-compassion
longTerm.description: Strong evidence shows self-compassion reduces shame and supports real behaviour change more effectively than self-criticism.

longTerm.title: Explore the roots
longTerm.description: If shame runs deep, therapy — including approaches like IFS or compassion-focused therapy — can help you understand and heal where it came from.

resources.title: Listening to shame
resources.whyHere: A landmark, accessible talk on shame and how vulnerability heals it.

resources.title: Self-compassion & self-forgiveness practices
resources.whyHere: Free guided exercises to soften shame and self-criticism.

resources.title: Compassion-focused therapy overview
resources.whyHere: Free resources on working with shame and self-criticism.

resources.title: The Happiness Trap (free worksheets)
resources.whyHere: Free ACT tools for unhooking from painful self-judgements.

matchPhrases: i feel like a bad person
